text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the seminal vesicles, dissecting out the vas deferens and seminal vesicles. The vas deferens is cauterized and divided, while the seminal vesicle is freed by dividing its lateral attachments. A plane is created between the seminal vesicles and prostate anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly by sharply dividing the Denonvilliers fascia. The opposite side's seminal vesicles and vas deferens are similarly dissected. A 3-0 PDS suture on a straight needle is passed through the anterior abdominal wall to elevate the bladder and seminal vesicles, preventing the bladder from obstructing the dissection field. The dissection continues between the seminal vesicle and prostate anteriorly and the rectum posteriorly, carefully avoiding the perirectal fat and rectum.

The surgeon dissects the lateral prostatic pedicles, clipping them with hemologs before dividing them to maintain a blood-free field. The seminal vesicle and prostate are carefully separated from the bladder, with gentle upward movements teasing the bladder away. The prostate becomes visible as the posterior bladder wall is dissected off its posterior surface. Dissection continues from posterior to lateral on both sides of the prostate, dividing the posterior prostato-vesical junction to deliver the prostate into the field.

The Foley catheter is visible after the division of the posterior prostato-vesical junction. Both lobes of the prostate are exposed as dissection proceeds laterally and anteriorly using cold scissors and electrocautery. The prostate is dissected laterally and separated from the levator muscles, with the anterior attachment to the bladder remaining. A stay suture is placed on the posterior bladder wall before dividing the anterior attachments. The anterior prostatocycle junction is divided, freeing the prostate from the bladder. Dissection continues to the prostatic apex, where the urethra is divided, preserving adequate length for urethrovicycle anastomosis. The specimen is dissected and delivered.

The surgeon performs the urethrovicycle anastomosis, starting at the twelve o'clock position using a 30 cm 3-0 V-lock suture. The continuous running suture is placed from twelve o'clock to six o'clock on one side, with the Foley catheter aiding in identifying the urethral lumen. The anastomosis is completed on the opposite side using a 15 cm V-lock suture, meeting at six o'clock where they are tied together for a watertight seal. Bleeding points are sutured to ensure hemostasis, completing the anastomosis.
